content.text: A React component is a function. Every time React needs to update what is displayed, it calls the component function again from the top. Any local variable created inside the function starts fresh — it has no memory of the previous call.

This is fine for things that never change — the component title, a computed string, a formatted date. But many UI elements need to remember something between renders: how many times a button was clicked, whether a modal is open, what text is in an input field. A plain local variable cannot do this.
Two things go wrong here. First, incrementing a local variable does not ask React to re-render, so the screen never updates. Second, even if React did re-render (for some other reason), it would call
State is a piece of data that React stores

, tied to a specific instance of the component in the rendered tree. Because it lives outside the function body, it survives across renders — the value from the previous render is available in the next one.

State also has a special superpower: when state changes, React schedules a re-render. The component function is called again, and this time it receives the new state value. The screen updates to reflect it.

attrs.alt: Diagram comparing a plain local variable (resets every render) vs useState (persists across renders and triggers re-renders)

content.text: Hook to add state to a function component. It takes an initial value and returns a pair: the current state value and a function to update it.
Now the component works as expected: each click calls
, React schedules a re-render, the component function runs again, and this time
returns the updated count (not 0 again) — React looked it up from storage.
Internally, React maintains a list of state slots for each component instance in the rendered tree (the fiber). Hooks are called in the same order on every render, so React can match each
call to its slot. This is why hooks must be called unconditionally at the top level of a component — not inside
Always call Hooks at the top level of your component, not inside conditions, loops, or nested functions. This guarantees the call order is stable across renders.
State Is Isolated Per Component Instance
Every component instance has its own state. Rendering the same component twice gives two independent pieces of state — they do not share a counter or a value:
This isolation is a feature. Components are self-contained. They do not accidentally share data just because they are the same function.
What Kinds of Values Belong in State
A good rule of thumb: put a value in state when
it changes over time and its new value should cause the UI to update
: the current value of a text input, whether a dropdown is open, the index of the selected tab, whether a checkbox is checked.
: the list of items fetched from an API, a loading flag, an error message.
: a countdown remaining, the current step of a multi-step form.
put values in state when they:
Can be computed from existing state or props (use a plain variable or
Do not affect what is rendered (use
for mutable values like timer IDs or DOM nodes).
Never change (put them in a module-level constant or pass them as props).
When React calls your component function, it gives you the state value as it was at the start of this render. Calling
does not change the current variable — it schedules a new render where
will hold the new value. Each render has its own constant snapshot of state.
This snapshot model prevents a whole category of subtle bugs where stale values from closures cause unexpected behavior. Each event handler closes over the state value from
render, not from some later render.
A component can have as many independent state variables as it needs. Each call to
Each state variable is independent. Updating
State vs Props: A Quick Recap
State and props both cause React to re-render a component when they change, but they have fundamentally different origins:
One of the simplest, most common uses of state is a boolean toggle — open/closed, expanded/collapsed, dark/light:
. Passing a function instead of a value reads the

state at the time the update is applied, rather than the snapshot from the current render. For simple toggles this makes no difference, but it is a good habit when the new value depends on the previous one.
Another classic: a text input whose value React controls. The input's
attribute is set from state; each keystroke updates the state, which re-renders the input with the new character:
: React is the single source of truth for what the input displays. Without the
handler updating state, the input would appear frozen —
would always equal the initial state.
The full state update cycle has four steps:
A user clicks, types, scrolls — or an async operation completes.
React records the new state value and marks the component as needing a re-render.
returns the new value from storage.
React compares the new output to the previous one (the diff) and updates only the parts of the DOM that changed.
calls within the same event handler into a single re-render. This is an optimization — you do not need to worry about it, but it means calling
in one handler causes one re-render, not two.
Common Mistakes When First Using State
Reading state immediately after setting it.
Because the snapshot does not update mid-render,
still logs the old value. The new value appears in the next render.
does not trigger a re-render. Never mutate the state variable directly.
, compute it inline rather than storing it as a third state variable. Redundant state creates synchronization bugs.
Calling setState inside the render body.
unconditionally in the function body creates an infinite loop: render → setState → render → setState → …. State updates belong in event handlers or

State can hold any JavaScript value — including objects and arrays. The same rule applies: never mutate the state value directly. Instead, create a new object or array with the changes applied and pass it to the setter.
to compare old and new state. Mutating an object in place keeps the same reference —
— so React sees no change and skips the re-render.
is used only on the very first render. On subsequent renders, React ignores it. If computing the initial value is expensive, pass a function instead of a value — React calls it exactly once:
State Lifting: Sharing State Between Siblings
Because state is private to the component that declares it, two sibling components cannot directly share a piece of state. The standard solution is to
to their nearest common ancestor, then pass it down as props:
receives it as a prop and calls the parent's setter when the user types.
receives a derived value. Both stay in sync because they both read from the single source of truth.
useState vs useRef: When Not to Use State
Not every mutable value needs state. If a value changes but those changes should
instead. Common examples: storing a timer ID, holding a reference to a DOM node, or tracking whether the component has mounted.
The interval ID is stored in a ref. Reading or writing
never schedules a re-render, which is exactly what we want — we only want the timer display to update, not the control buttons.
Resetting State by Changing the Key
State is tied to the component's position in the tree. If you need to completely reset a component's state when a prop changes, give it a new
. React unmounts the old instance and mounts a new one — state starts fresh from the initial value:
is the idiomatic way to reset a subtree of state. It is cleaner than managing a
that calls multiple setters back to their initial values.
Batching: Multiple State Updates in One Render
Since React 18, all state updates — even those triggered inside
, or native event handlers — are automatically batched. Calling multiple setters in sequence still results in one re-render:

Batching is an optimization you generally do not need to think about. The key takeaway: React defers re-renders to the end of the current event, so within one event handler you can call as many setters as you need and pay the cost of only one render.
React Testing Library makes it straightforward to test state-driven behavior: simulate the user action and assert the resulting UI, without ever touching state directly.

Testing state through the UI rather than implementation details makes tests resilient to refactoring. If you later move count into a custom hook or context, the test still passes because it only checks what the user sees.
Components are functions; local variables reset on every call. State is stored outside the function by React and survives across renders.
. Call the setter to request a new value and a re-render.
Each component instance has independent state — the same component rendered twice has two separate state slots.
State is a snapshot per render. The current render's state variable does not change when you call setState — the update takes effect in the next render.
Use state for values that change over time and whose changes should update the UI. Prefer plain variables for derived values and
for mutable values that do not need to trigger renders.
